title: Swiggy Delivery Stair Climb
description: A Swiggy delivery executive must climb `n` stairs to reach a customer's flat. Each step they can climb either 1 or 2 stairs at a time. Return the total number of distinct ways to reach the top.
hints: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2) — classic Fibonacci recurrence.
Base: ways(0)=1, ways(1)=1.
Two variables enough — O(1) space.

title: Razorpay K-Step Subscription Ladder
description: Razorpay offers a tiered subscription with `n` levels. From any level a customer can upgrade by 1, 2, ..., up to `k` levels in one move. Starting at level 0, return the number of distinct upgrade paths to reach exactly level `n`.
constraints: 1 <= k <= n or k = 1 when n = 0
hints: Use a running window sum to avoid the inner k-loop — O(n).
Base dp[0] = 1.

title: Swiggy Skip-Last-Two Climb
description: A delivery rider can climb stairs in steps of 1 or 2, but a rule forbids two consecutive 2-steps (the last two moves can't both be 2). Given `n`, return the number of distinct climbing sequences obeying this rule.

examples.explanation: 1111, 112, 121, 211, 22 is BANNED (two 2s consecutively allowed only with one between), wait re-check. Sequences: 1111, 112, 121, 211, 22 — 22 has the only two-step pair adjacent so it's banned; valid = 4? Actually rule says two consecutive 2-steps not allowed, 22 has them adjacent, so it's banned; the count is 4.

hints: State: dp[i][last] where last ∈ {1,2} indicates last step size.
Transitions: from last=1 you can step 1 or 2; from last=2 you can only step 1.
Roll into two scalars for O(1) space.

title: Flipkart Warehouse Row Robber
description: A thief is planning to loot Flipkart's warehouse, where boxes are arranged linearly. Each box `nums[i]` has goods of value. Adjacent boxes are wired with a shared alarm: looting two adjacent boxes triggers it. Return the max value lootable without triggering the alarm.
hints: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]) — skip or take.
Base dp[0]=nums[0], dp[1]=max(nums[0],nums[1]).
Two scalars → O(1) space.

title: Paytm Cashback Min Coins
description: Paytm gives cashback in coins of fixed denominations `coins`. Given a target amount, return the minimum number of coins needed to make that amount exactly. If impossible, return -1. You have unlimited supply of each denomination.
hints: dp[a] = min over c in coins of (dp[a-c] + 1).
Initialise dp[0]=0, others = Infinity.
Skip coins larger than current amount.

title: Razorpay Subscription Plan Combinations
description: Razorpay subscription plans cost certain integer amounts (`plans` array). A customer wants to spend exactly `amount` total by buying any combination (with repetition) of plans. Order doesn't matter (combinations, not permutations). Return the number of distinct combinations.
hints: Outer loop on coins, inner on amount — order matters here!
dp[a] += dp[a - coin].
Outer-on-amount would count permutations instead.

title: PhonePe Merchant Tag Word Break
description: PhonePe's merchant onboarding strips spaces from tags before storing. Given the stripped tag `s` and a dictionary `words` of valid lowercase words, return `true` if `s` can be segmented into a sequence of one or more dictionary words.
hints: dp[i] = true if s[0..i-1] can be segmented.
For each i, look at each j < i: if dp[j] && s.substring(j,i) in dict, set dp[i]=true.
Use a Set for O(1) word lookup.

title: Ola Longest Increasing Trip Streak
description: Ola tracks the rating each driver received per trip in a day, in order: `ratings[i]`. A 'streak' is a subsequence (not necessarily contiguous) whose ratings are strictly increasing. Return the length of the longest such streak.

examples.explanation: 1,4,5,9 length 4 or 1,4,5,6 length 4? Actual LIS: 1,4,5,9 → 4. Let me re-check: 3,4,5,9 → 4; 1,4,5,9 → 4; 1,4,5,6 → 4; longest is 4. Output 4.

hints: O(n^2) DP: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) for j<i with ratings[j]<ratings[i].
O(n log n) patience sort: maintain tails array; binary search insertion point.
Strict increasing → use lower_bound, replace at insertion point.

title: Flipkart Max Sale Subarray
description: Flipkart logs hourly profit/loss as integers (negative = loss) across the day. Find the contiguous time window with the highest total profit. Return that maximum sum (Kadane's).
hints: Kadane: cur = max(num, cur + num); best = max(best, cur).
Initialise both with nums[0].
All-negative case: best is the largest single element.

title: Swiggy Decode Customer PIN
description: Swiggy stores customer PINs encoded with a→1, b→2, ..., z→26. Given a digit string `s`, return the number of ways to decode it back into letters. A decoding is valid if every used number is in 1..26 (so '0' alone is invalid; '10', '20' are valid; '27', '00', '30' as a unit are invalid).
hints: dp[i] = ways to decode prefix of length i.

title: Amazon Partition Equal Loot
description: Two Amazon delivery agents want to fairly split a pile of parcels by weight (`weights` of positive integers): each agent takes some parcels so the two halves have equal total weight. Return `true` if a 50-50 split is possible.
hints: Total odd → return false.
Subset-sum DP: bool dp[s] = can we hit sum s?
Iterate weights; for each, update dp from high to low to keep 0/1 knapsack semantics.

title: Flipkart Sale-Day One Transaction
description: Flipkart Big Billion Days: stock prices for `n` consecutive hours given as `prices`. You may buy once and sell once (sell strictly after buy). Return the max profit. If no profit is possible, return 0.
hints: Track running min so far; for each day compute price - minSoFar.
Update best profit.
Single pass O(n), O(1) space.

title: Razorpay Stock with Cooldown
description: Razorpay's trading bot can buy/sell unlimited times, but after each SELL, there's a 1-day cooldown (cannot buy on the day right after a sell). Given `prices`, return max total profit.
hints: Three states per day: hold, sold (just sold today, must cooldown next), rest (idle, free to buy).
Transitions: hold = max(prevHold, prevRest - price); sold = prevHold + price; rest = max(prevRest, prevSold).
Roll into 3 scalars.

title: PhonePe Stock with Transaction Fee
description: PhonePe charges a flat `fee` per round-trip transaction (paid on each sell). Given `prices` and `fee`, return the max profit with unlimited transactions.
hints: Two states: hold, cash.
cash = max(cash, hold + price - fee).
hold = max(hold, cash - price).

title: Flipkart Jump Game Reachability
description: A robot starts at index 0 of `nums`. From any index `i`, it can jump to any index in `[i+1, i + nums[i]]`. Return `true` iff it can reach the last index.
hints: Greedy: track furthest reachable index so far.
If i > maxReach, you're stuck. Otherwise update maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]).
DP: dp[i] = true if reachable.

title: Ola Min Trip Hops
description: From index 0 of `nums`, hop forward up to `nums[i]` steps. Return the minimum number of hops to reach the last index. Assume reachability (last index is always reachable).
hints: Greedy/BFS-like: track current jump's farthest, when you exhaust it increment jumps and update farthest.
currentEnd, farthest variables.
DP O(n^2): dp[i] = min over reachable j of dp[j]+1.

title: Amazon Max Product Subarray
description: Amazon analytics multiplies hourly factor signals (signed integers in `nums`). Find the contiguous subarray with the maximum product and return that product.
hints: Negative * negative = positive: track BOTH max and min ending at i.
On hitting a negative, swap roles before computing.
Reset when 0 (effectively starts fresh).

title: Razorpay Min Perfect Squares Sum
description: Razorpay's loyalty engine awards points only when the redemption amount equals a sum of perfect squares (1, 4, 9, 16, ...). Given target `n`, return the minimum count of perfect squares summing to `n` (squares may repeat).
hints: dp[i] = min over k where k*k <= i of dp[i - k*k] + 1.
Initialise dp[0]=0.
Lagrange's four-square theorem: answer is always <= 4.

title: Amazon Integer Break Max Product
description: Given integer `n >= 2`, break it into a sum of at least 2 positive integers and maximize their product. Return that maximum product.
hints: dp[i] = max over j in 1..i-1 of max(j*(i-j), j*dp[i-j]).
Base dp[2]=1.
Math: prefer breaking into 3s once n >= 4.

title: PhonePe Buy/Sell at Most K Transactions
description: PhonePe trader can perform at most `k` buy-sell pairs (transactions) on `prices`. A new transaction can only start after the previous one closes. Return the max total profit.
hints: buy[j] = max(buy[j], sell[j-1] - price); sell[j] = max(sell[j], buy[j] + price).
Iterate prices outer, j from 1..k inner.
If k >= n/2, behaves like unlimited transactions.

title: Ola Circular Stand House Robber
description: Ola driver lounges are arranged in a CIRCLE — boxes 0 and n-1 are now adjacent (shared alarm). All other rules from linear robber stand: no two adjacent looted. Given `nums`, return max loot.

examples.explanation: Cannot take both 0 and 2; pick 3 alone.

hints: Run linear robber twice: once excluding first index, once excluding last index.
Return the max of the two.
Edge case: n=1 → nums[0].

title: Amazon Min Cost Color Houses
description: Amazon has `n` warehouse buildings to paint, each in one of 3 colors (red/blue/green). `costs[i]` is a 3-element array [costRed, costBlue, costGreen] for building `i`. Two adjacent buildings must NOT share a color. Return the minimum total cost.
hints: Three running scalars for the 3 colors.
newRed = costs[i][0] + min(prevBlue, prevGreen). Similarly for others.
Final answer = min of three at end.

title: Razorpay Cashback Max Non-Adjacent Sum
description: Razorpay offers `n` cashback offers, but you can claim non-adjacent ones (cooldown rules). Given `offers` array of integer values (could be 0), return the max sum of values claimed such that no two claimed indices are adjacent.
hints: Same as house robber.
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + offers[i]).
O(1) space with two scalars.

title: Swiggy Variable Jump Steps
description: Swiggy navigation game: on a 1-D path of length `n`, starting at index 0, the rider can jump exactly steps from `allowed` (a set of positive integers) at each move. Return the minimum number of jumps to land at index n-1 exactly. If impossible, return -1.

examples.explanation: Cannot land on 3 from 0 with only step 2.

hints: BFS-like DP: dp[i] = min jumps to reach i.
dp[i] = min over s in allowed of dp[i-s] + 1, if dp[i-s] valid.
Initialise dp[0]=0, others = Infinity.

title: Amazon Decode Ways with Wildcard
description: Amazon variant of decode ways: digit string `s` may also contain '*', which represents any single digit 1-9. Return the number of ways to decode (mod 10^9+7). a→1..z→26 mapping; '0' alone is invalid; '*' is never '0'.

examples.explanation: * as 0..9 single = 9 if non-zero each, plus pair 10..19 = 9 more, but pair 10 is 1 valid, etc. Actual count: 18.

hints: Same DP as decode ways but each digit position has multiple possibilities.
Single decode contributions: '*' gives 9, '0' gives 0, else 1.
Two-digit decode contributions: enumerate pairs (* with digit, digit with *, ** etc.).

title: Flipkart Number of LIS
description: Given Flipkart trip ratings array `nums`, return the number of longest strictly-increasing subsequences (LIS) — count, not the LIS itself.
hints: Two arrays: len[i] = LIS length ending at i; cnt[i] = number of such LISes.
For each j < i with nums[j] < nums[i]: if len[j]+1 > len[i], reset; if equal, accumulate.
Sum cnt[i] over all i with len[i] == max len.

title: Amazon Min Deletions for Sorted Order
description: Amazon's recommendation feed has items with rank scores in `nums`. To make it strictly increasing, we delete a minimum number of items. Return that minimum number of deletions. (Equivalent to n - LIS.)

examples.explanation: LIS length is 4 (1,4,5,9 or similar), so deletions = 8-4 = 4.

hints: Answer = n - LIS(nums) (strict).
Use O(n log n) patience-sort LIS.
Strict: lower_bound on tails.
